title: 2025/10/09 - FD
displayTitle: 2025-10-09 - Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee - Defence (Workforce) Amendment Bill
committee: Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee
description: Thursday 09 October 2025 | Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee Defence (Workforce) Amendment Bill New Zealand Council of Trade Unions Richard Wagstaff Max Marshall New Zealand Public Service Association Te Pukenga Here Tikanga Mahi (PSA) Fleur Fitzsimons, National Secretary Andrew McCauley, Policy Advisor Fleur Fitzsimons, National Secretary
descriptionSummary: Defence (Workforce) Amendment Bill. New Zealand Council of Trade Unions. Richard Wagstaff

chunks.text: All right. Good morning. We have first of all, the New Zealand Council of Trade Union. Welcome along. Today, we're hearing submissions on the defense workforce, me, Minville. We're live streaming that, and we've received your written submission. We have 10 minutes set aside for your oral submission today and commissions.

We'll hand straight over to you to speak to us. Thank you. Thank, thank you, chair. Uh, marina. My name is Richard Wesoff. I'm the President of the Council of Trade Union, and beside me is, is Gail Algoa. He is our legal officer. I'll make some opening remarks and then I'll hand it over to Gail to make some more, um, important points. I think in broad terms, um, we are deeply, um, not just opposed to this, but, but actually quite surprised by it.

You know, we've had a long period of industrial relations changes, um, ups and ebbs and flows and so on, but not in any time in decades have we seen a bill like this, which goes to the heart of people's democratic right to strike. Um, the existing arrangements, the status quo, as far as I can see, weren't causing any problems. Were just working. They, they, they enabled, um, an emergency situations, the deployment of the military, um, in very rare situations.

And here we have this legislation come proposed, being proposed, and we can't see, well, if this was the answer, what was the problem? Where are the examples of a need to, of, to change what we had in place? We can't see them. What we did have was a workforce who has offered zero at the bargaining table, tried to do something about it, and there seems to be a massive overage to slam back with a legislation like this, which will severe severely curtail their, uh, opportunities and rights and, and bargaining.

So, in broad terms, you know, this one seems really out there to me, uh, as a CGU President to go after these fundamental rights in a country that we like to proudly consider a liberal democracy that upholds, you know, basic fundamental democratic freedoms. I'll hand over to Gale to go into some points here. Um, thank you Richard. And, um, thank you, uh, committee for this opportunity to submit on this bill.

Um, as you've acknowledged, I, I know that the, the committee has received a copy of a written submission, so I won't go into that document in a great deal of detail. Um, one thing I do want to do is to acknowledge and endorse the submission of the Public Service Association, and I want to highlight that their submission stands on the insights of the workers who will be impacted by this bill and should be given the foremost consideration by this committee.

Um, there are a few points that I want to sort, sort of, um, draw out and highlight, uh, for the committee today. Um, the first point is the issue, uh, of executive overstep. So this bill expands the use of a discretionary, discretionary executive powers without parliamentary oversight.

Clause four extends the period for which armed forces can provide public services during industrial disputes from 14 to 13 days, or if Parliament is not sitting the next sitting day. This is a huge expansion on what has been a carefully ring-fenced and scrutinized power. In effect, if sitting days are taken into account, the minister may replace striking or locked up workers with military personnel for up to 40 days at a time, and well in excess of a hundred days.

If the impact of election cycles are taken into account. Um, this is not, clearly not in our view, a measure to support the provision of needed public services. This is a provision to crush the industrial power of, uh, workers and to prolong industrial disputes. Clause five allows the minister to deploy armed forces to cover civil staff, uh, to a broad range of circumstances well beyond, um, civil emergency, which is the threshold that exists in the Principal Act at the moment.

Um, under the bill, the minister may use this power, uh, with only a requirement to inform the house, um, and may unilaterally determine or extend the timeframe, uh, for the deployment of armed forces. Um, this, these are significant expansions of executive rights at the expense of both parliamentary and industrial democracy.

Another issue that we want to highlight for the committee is the impact that this will have on the New Zealand Defense Force, um, calling the armed forces to cover work. The work of striking or locked out workers is an aggressive and controversial industrial tactic, and it is for this reason that it's very carefully prescribed in legislation.

Um, this is a step towards normalizing that kind of tactic. And moving towards normalizing such things will diminish the reputation of the Defense Force and the government, and it will place the military personnel who are already stretched and under-resourced in an unfair position of having to, of being used to undermine the rights of working people.

Um, it is not right, it is not their job to cushion employers from having to deal with the effects of industrial disputes. Um, and it's not their role. So, um, that's, that's something that we want to emphasize very strongly in this submission. Um, if an employer wants to avoid the acute effects of industrial action, there are other options available to them.

Primarily, they can listen to their workers, they can negotiate and use the tools that are available to employers under industrial law. The message being sent to the public service and to the civil, uh, civil staff of Defense Force is very dire. Um, you are, you're telling them that they're replaceable, that they can be ignored, and that the industrial tactics can be bypassed.

If this bill is passed in our submission, it'll represent a depressing turn in the status of working people in this country country. And so, for those reasons and for the reasons outlined in our written submission, uh, we strongly recommend that this, uh, committee take a view that this bill should not progress any further.

Um, thank you. We're happy to take any, any questions. Alright. Thank you very much for your submission. Uh, first question, Tim Costley. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Frankly, I'm pretty appalled at this submission, um, and the overtly dramaticized language and just factual inaccuracies all the way through it, sitting here trying to tell us that civilians are often zero at the bargaining table went for year after year.

Over the last few years, they have received pay rises and those that turn up in uniform haven't, uh, is, is frankly more than disappointing. Going through your, in your submission, and I'd like to hear what you are basing some of this on when you talk about subpart for the giving, the Prime Minister, um, powers to go and help if there's a civil emergency.

Subpart four is specifically on advice from the Commissioner of Police for something like a counter terrorist incident. I'm, I'm assuming you've actually read this act. Um, if you keep working through it, you, you don't seem to understand that this is not about using the military to cover civil action, for example, uh, protesting prison guards.

I think that's completely valid to talk about that oversight, and that's what, not just my opinion, but advice suggests part two of, of, of, uh, section nine was always intended for, this is about an internal, uh, a person whose day job as a security, uh, force, uh, sailor, soldier, airmen in the military, filling the role of a civilian security guard in the av, uh, Navy, the army, the Air Force doing the job they would do anyway.

And, and, and in that regard, the Defense Force would be the only place, the, the only place I can find any evidence of where chief executive is not able to disperse and deploy his forces internally, uh, as he sees fit. It. It is a completely different situation to what you are trying to make this out to be.

And I'd like to understand how you've even, uh, got to this and, and how a 14 day versus a thirsty day, uh, approval period, which is still higher than any other chief executive has to go through any chief executive in the public service. How would that make a difference to the, to the, to the rights of civilian employees who frankly from the ones I've worked with, um, come to work and do an amazing job in our defense force with a spirit of service, not with trying to hold, uh, the defense force over a barrel, um, in,

in the manner that you described. So I, I'd appreciate where, how you're even reading this to be anything like what it's, Yeah. Um, look, it's, that's a very, uh, with respect, a very, um, uh, protracted and difficult to understand, uh, question. I think you've, uh, uh, Well, let's start with subpart four, the act.

Do you understand subpar four, the act only applies in specifically says it's on advice from the Commissioner of Police? Yes. That's for civil emergency. So how would that, do you understand? Do you understand the bill? No, I'm trying to, yeah. How would that help? I'm trying to answer your question. That's what's already provided in the act. Do you understand that this bill changes that and expands that?

No, it doesn't. This act is specifically about things like national security. Uh, it's about things like guarding the front gate of a military base. 'cause that's kind of important. It's about being able to get ships and aircraft deployed for a humanitarian crisis in the Pacific. It is not about counter-terrorist situations or civil defense emergencies. This, this, this act establishes a, a threshold of reasonableness for the, for the, um, for the minister to apply.

The minister's not bound to follow any advice, not bound to be, um, directed by any other, uh, authority. The minister's supposed to listen, but this is actually a discretion. And, uh, I think if you can't realize that, I think we need to go through the, you need to go through the, I'm sorry. You don't think it's reasonable that we would guard the front gate of a military base?

Um, well, well, uh, isn't that something that military personnel do? Uh, anyway. No, That's exactly what we went to Parliament for last year to cover Christmas. So You get, you get, you get civil def defense, you get civilian, um, civilian personnel to guard military bases. Is that what you're telling me? Yes. Civilian personnel, they'll sit on the front gate of military bases as to military personnel last Christmas.

Uh, and they guard, we had to go to parliament to get military personnel to be able to cover civilians on the front gate. And that's what, that's what people are opposing here. So what, so what's the problem with that? This is exactly right. Currently we have settings which enable those situations to be, uh, addressed by going to Parliament. And we are saying that these existing, the existing status quo works.

You can do this in emergency circumstances, but it's an emergency circumstance that you should only take, you know, with a, with a lot of caution because you're interfering with basic fundamental civil rights here in industrial rights. So there should be a very high threshold and a mechanism that that can be applied, but only in extreme circumstances you've used that mechanism. It seems to be the justification here is because the industrial action once acutely felt well, that's what industrial action's meant to do,

otherwise, you never get through a dispute. You need to have a balance of, of power continue. Uh, and that's basically what, what we're saying here, what's wrong with what you've got. It's right, it that makes it hard to do, but it's, it's doable in extreme circumstances. Mm-hmm. It's very rare for this, for this force to take industrial action. It's only when they're provoked by minimal office at the table, but for most of the time, they get a settlement, uh, and do so in good faith.

And we would say that the current settings are providing for that. Once the employer knows they have a, a much easier mechanism to deny them effective industrial action, I think getting those, uh, settlements will be much, much harder. Yeah, I think Mr. Coley's point is that, uh, whilst that's reasonable and most situations, the unique situations of the extended Christmas period or an election for example, provides such an extended timeframe, that that would not be possible under the existing legislation to allow for

that cover to continue. And it is a critical function. It must continue. So. All right. Look, thank you very much for your time today. Really appreciate that. We'll move now on to the next submission from Max Marshall. Thank you.

Uh, good morning. Good morning, Ms. Marshall. Hi. Thank you very much for coming along today. Appreciate your time. We've received your written submission and we have five minutes set down for your oral submission today, including questions, and I'll hand straight over to you. Great. Thank you very much. Um, good morning, chair and panel. I believe that the policy is taken over from the Policy Pro problem it was meant to solve.

If I look, if I looked at armed forces, it has a special relationship unlike any other arm of the state with the government and the constitutional framework. Indeed, it, indeed it is the key to the maintenance of a liberal democracy. The defense force has not been immune to the changes of the last 40 years of this, the move towards managerialism and the thought and designation of people as self interested agents, red reduction of the government and the defense relationship and business to these reductive terms that of owner

and and purchaser. The framing of activities into outputs and inputs has led to this present problem. The government's pro promotion of defense forces towards some of the lengths with commercial partners and lending the Defense Force name to promote private manufacturers seems at odds with the central aim of the Defense Force stated above.

If I thought the move to use civilian workforce was flawed, flawed 40 years ago, I think it is equally flawed to put the Defense Force personnel into the role to replace them in an industrial action. Why is this? Both of these problems stem from the same issue.

Einstein said, we cannot solve a problem At the same level of thinking, the minister has said, the Defense force cannot work without the right equipment and conditions. I agree, if we do not have the right conditions, what I mean by conditions is the shift in the mentality of not looking at a person as a burden, but as an investment.

In the same time, uh, they will be looking at the organization with their own questions rather than looking at the upfront value a person could contribute. It is about the value gain from the organization through retention and this value which has gained over a lifetime. Here, I'm making reference to meaning because it is important when people see their value only or for what they'll get out when they leave, not what they, they give to the institution.

This, this is, this shows the attitude which contractual has created. Upgrading our force means much more than just equipment. It is trahan changing the organ organization. So it will mutually attract people who share the vision and want to invest their lives in contributing to this very important work over many other choices they could make.

This requires an investment, a long-term investment by person and the investment of the institution. And that person, which contractual blocks the Defense force structure, does not stifle creativity, innovation, and intellectual curiosity. It gives it permission.

Um, they would be producing solutions. They could have applications if they were working in a, in the private sector, but who benefit instead their state and the public. The question of your renu remuneration is difficult and something I'm not prepared to give any opinion on. In the interim, they're going to want to have a partner and a family.

Do we want them to struggle with the question each month of how they want? They are going to make the mortgage repayment. It is a, a distraction. I do not want to, I, I, I want, uh, it is not a distraction I want to give them from the work on the particular issue they have been tasked with. I know that this will take a huge shift, but the last 40 years have proved, if anything, something cannot be pared down to business, a business term reference.

The consideration we have where our focus is taken up by concerns around industrial action is trivial by comparison. But that's where we are today. All right. Thank you, Ms. Marshall. A question from the Honorable Damien. Uh, look, thank, thank you very much for your submission here. Look, just a little bit confused here because I, you've said, um, uh, all administration should be done by active personnel.

I appreciate your position. Yes. What I can't understand is you're saying that, but I do not want the government to stop using third party contractor to undertake special support functions. Exact example, catering. Uh, that's not surely the core catering food services for military personnel, though, are you? Oh, I have spoken to your honorable, um, co, uh, colleague, um, uh, um, penny Henry about this issue.

He's, he's to told me, um, this too, um, I guess it's a, it is a, a practicals, um, situation to, to make, but, um, I, I wouldn't, I I, I'd want to, um, u use it in a, um, I I'm sure, I'm sure, I'm sure that, that there's a, it's re reasonable to, to allow people to, to do that if they ne this, if it's necessary.

I, I, I guess my view is that, you know, the Army marches on the stomach has been known for centuries. I guess if there's ever a critical issue, it would be the provision of food. So, But the, i I mean, you could actually, I mean, so why we have a, why couldn't we increase the workforce to go and do that?

Why couldn't we? Um, why, um, couldn't we, um, not, not if that's what, what's needed. The, the having having, um, the, there's transitional costs and involved in ha having a, having contract tractors do do that. And you could say there's benefit in having and an employment.

Uh, I think I've always thought that having that, having these, having big, big, these big large in public institutions were, um, ways of, uh, um, um, soaking up of, um, uh, managing a lot of unemployment. A lot of unemployment, I think out there was, was, was done by many of these things, the hospitals, the, the railways.

Um, yeah, I'm sorry if I sound o old, old school, but that's, I think things were better then. I'm not sure about how, what I think about society today. All right. Thank you very much for your time, Mr. Marshall. Really appreciate your submission. Cheers. Okay. We'll move now to the New Zealand Public Service Association.
Good morning.
I'm sorry, we can't hear you.

Sorry. Is that better? Yes, we can hear you now. Thank you very much. All right. We have 10 minutes set aside for your submission this morning. We've received your written submission. Uh, we welcome your comments in support of that, uh, and I'm sure there'll be questions from members as well. Thank you. Cool. Thanks very much. And I'm just being joined by flu for Simons, too. Hey, everyone, and thanks so much for having us along today.

It's a very important issue for the PSA. We have a thousand members around about working as civilian personnel in the New Zealand Defense Force for many years. We have bargained collective agreements for these workers, and we do believe, and it's been, uh, pretty well canvassed, that this bill is a result of effective industrial action, which civilian members of the New Zealand Defense Force took, uh, at the end of the year before last and early this year, that industrial action took place in an important context.

The context was that the head of the Defense Force announced to all civilian personnel that there would be a zero pay offer. Well, it wasn't a pay offer, it was a decision. It was presented to everybody as you will be paid no more than you are. Now, that was done to the civilian personnel, but also publicly.

Now in New Zealand, employers aren't entitled to simply announce what the pay rise will or won't be. They need to sit down and try and in good faith, agree it with the workforce. What we saw when it came to the New Zealand Defense Force was simply an announcement. Now, that is not the way to conduct industrial relations.

We have had a long and difficult relationship with the Defense Force, but I just wanna acknowledge right now that there are extensive efforts by senior personnel, both military and civilian and senior personnel, uh, and delegates and, and union officials to try and put the relationship on a better footing.

So please, none of my comments are about the work that is going in from both sides now to try and rectify some of the problems that have occurred in the past that have undermined the terms and conditions of civilian personnel. But I do just wanna reflect on that because I think it's very relevant to this piece of proposed legislation.

In the past, the PSA had to take litigation for the New Zealand Defense Force to even agree to bargain pay with us. Now, it wasn't even in this round of bargaining that was in previous bargaining. We had to take them to the Employment Relations Authority and say, you are required to bargain pay with CPSA.

They are entitled to be in the room. They are entitled to have a position on behalf of their civilian members at the New Zealand Defense Force. We then had to take further litigation when the Defense Force deliberately offered non-union members more in an attempt to undermine the bargaining. And then, as I said, in this round of bargaining, we had a situation where the chief of the Defense Force simply announced that there wouldn't be a pay increase.

Now, there was then a very, uh, protracted industrial dispute, and the government has already said that it reduced, uh, it introduced the partial strikes deduction, partly in response to this industrial action. We, in the end, got a settlement of that dispute. We only got a settlement because that industrial action was disruptive because the government had to pass resolutions because those Defense Force civilian personnel took the very brave and difficult decision to continue taking industrial action to get a better outcome for them and their families.

And I just wanna talk about who these civilian personnel are. They are absolutely critical people to the combat ready nature of the New Zealand Defense Force. The New Zealand Defense Force is only able to operate well because of the dedication of civilian personnel. They include people like diesel engineers, security guards, IT specialists, and the administrators who make sure that the military are able to serve properly, both here and overseas, who do all the work in the background.

They're absolutely critical to the backbone of the New Zealand Defense Force, and they should be valued, and their ability to take industrial action should be celebrated because they needed to, to achieve things for them and their families. And the most recent industrial dispute is a clear example of that. They were offered nothing, including on the radio by the chief of the Defense Force.

They took a series of industrial action, and in the end, we got a thousand dollars to every worker, and also a commitment that trades based roles would be properly valued and that the union and the employer would work on them. None of that was possible except for the fact that we were able to undertake a campaign of industrial action because civilian defense personnel had had enough.

They were constantly told that they weren't valuable, that their value was, was to be determined by the employer, not to be agreed between them and the union. So I just want to break and, and even then, after all the industrial action and all the disruption that occurred, it was only able to take place because we went to facilitation with the Employment Relations Authority over a period of three days in a confidential setting and agreed an outcome.

We also agreed to rebuild the relationship between the civilian personnel and the Defense Force. And I just wanna say as well, that these personnel had incredible support from their military colleagues. They said they do not want to be undermining them in their strike action.

They said that privately to us, they, that the whole approach of using the military as replacement labor is incredibly divisive. And that is particularly problematic in a defense force. A defense force needs to act as one. It's part of the strength of it. This bill is damaging, it's divisive and it's dangerous, and we ask that you don't proceed with it.

What we want to do is have a equal footing relationship. We want to be respected when we come and advocate on behalf of civilian personnel to the employer. We don't want to have to take disruptive industrial action. But that will keep happening if the Chief of the Defense Force keeps announcing zero payoffs.

And look, I'm hopeful that won't happen in the future given the improved relationship we have after this industrial action. But I just wanna also say that this government's record and defense is very problematic. We've lost lots of civilian personnel. 141 of them took voluntary redundancy. A whole bunch more have been restructured out, vacancies haven't been filled.

The Defense Force civilian personnel are being starved of their ability to properly operate in the Defense Force and to be valued and respected. Um, we are worried that the practical implications of this bill will be very problematic as well. There isn't, uh, the appropriate training for military personnel to do many of the civilian personnel tasks.

We are worried that there's less parliamentary scrutiny. But at the end of the day, what we are really worried about is that this undermines the ability of civilian defense personnel to be properly valued and to stand up for themselves and their families. And I spoke to them individually, many of them in advance of our facilitation. And what they told me is that their families were sacrificing their own wellbeing because they were committed to the defense force.

I was astonished at the low pay that we were expecting civilian personnel to operate in our defense force on. So they need all the help they can get in bargaining better from the Defense Force, and this takes away from that. All right. And very, thank you very much appreciate your submission question from Tim Coston. Yeah, can I just, just, just to understand on page one, and you make the point, I think you've articulated your point quite well as have others, that, um, by using military personnel to backfill these positions

during industrial action, it's sort of, uh, impinges or, or, or it li it limits the impact of that, that the industrial election might otherwise have. But then on page four, in your submission, when you talk about the bill being detrimental to the NCDF, you say in these cases, military personnel would need to drop their own duties with the cascading effect on the operation of the NCDF.

I mean, that doesn't that illustrate the exact point that actually using military personnel, say a security, uh, uh, per personnel to cover a civilian security force, it does actually still have a meaningful impact the industrial action. It's just that there are some issues that are critical. And the examples we've heard here, we're getting a frigate away on a six month deployment.

Um, it was, uh, guarding the main, the main gate to an ammo dump or to a military base. So, so yes, this power enables the CDF to protect those core functions, but it still has a meaningful impact, doesn't it? I mean, your own submission says that, Well, um, it sounds like you've got a future as a union official, because we will try and find ways for it to still have a meaningful impact.

But with the, in the introduction of, um, the partial strikes legislation and this, it is deliberately designed for it to have less of an impact. And what happens in industrial disputes is the less of an impact that a strike has, the less likely the employer is to take it seriously and settle. So we want all the levers, and actually, government has a role as well, because we wouldn't be in industrial disputes if the military was funded properly in the first place.

Can, can I just clarify, aside from the, you know, biggest funding increase ever for defense, but can I just clarify, so how would this be different? Say, when I was at high school at Palmy Boys High, that most of the staff there didn't believe in striking and so on strike days, I still had to go to school, which I was gutted about. But what they did is they just got relievers into cover.

The few teachers that did strike, they were able to use their assets. Sometimes on a company coast, we have this horrible thing called bus replacements. Maybe the train drivers go on strike, but they replaced them with buses. How, how is this this different? Of course, it will have some effect, but it has lesser and lesser effect, and that's what your government is deliberately designing into legislation industrial action that is less effective.

And look, the, the, the harsh reality is that industrial action is meant to be disruptive. It's meant to send a clear message. And look, I'll tell you, it's not easy for a thousand civilian personnel to decide to go on strike of all workers. They do not take that decision lightly at all. But when they do it, we should listen.

And while your government has made significant investment in defense that has not been in civilian personnel, hundreds of civilian personnel have left the defense force either through voluntary redundancy or forced dismissals because of funding cuts by your government. So I won't have it you saying that you've got this record investment, it certainly hasn't been into civilian personnel, and we had to fight and fight to get them.

Uh, and not even that, that effect, uh, increase that much of a pay increase. So, you know, and now you want to take away the last remaining bits of power that they have in an industrial dispute. The best thing you could do is recognize them and value them properly. Thank You. We'll just fit in one final question from Tiano to your honor. Ki thank you very much for your, uh, very detailed, um, uh, submission.

Uh, you, you talked earlier about the, the relationship between, uh, military staff and civilian staff and, and, and the impact that there's, that, that this is having on them. Uh, how, how do you see the impact of this bill on that relationship given, um, when there are, when there are industrial disputes, you need to be able to actually sit down and have a, have a talk and have a discussions to actually move things forward.

What will the, what will the impact in your view of this bill be on that relationship? Yeah, thanks for the question. 'cause I do think it's an important one. We need the defense force to operate strongly as one. And what bringing in these strike breakers will do is add tension and division to the way that the military operates.

And in many cases, that tension and division will last long after the dispute has been settled because relationships will be frayed and it will be very difficult. Civilian personnel will see some of the strike breakers as undermining the industrial action. And what the message we've had from the military personnel is that they don't want to be put in that position.

Some of them have really put their neck out and expressed to civilian personnel that they're deeply uncomfortable with being asked to do this. So it's gonna be divisive, there'll be tensions, and they may last many, many years long after the industrial dispute is settled. All right. Thank you very much for your time today.
Appreciate it. Thank you. Uh, our final submitter is not joining us today. So that brings an end to the public submissions on the Defense Workforce Amendment.
